access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for afw1075

entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for afw1075

rights.basis: The pinned CME root LICENSE contains the Unlicense and dedicates 'software' to the public domain. It does not expressly establish that the repository has authority to dedicate inherited U-M XML, transcription, or markup, so the Unlicense assertion is recorded without treating it as conclusive for this data artifact.
rights.component: Pinned CME submodule XML input, including inherited U-M transcription and markup
notes: The current U-M item-level public-domain / NoC-US assertion is recorded separately on the digital-encoding entity.

access.format: HTML digital text; the legacy XML records historically linked page images
notes: Public access to the text does not establish current retrievability of the complete historical TIFF set.

entities.description: A 2006 uncorrected OCR text encoded at Level 1 under the TEI in Libraries guidelines, with links to 600 dpi page images. The public interface identifies the item as public domain and applies No Copyright - United States.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AFW1075.0001.001

rights.basis: The current U-M item identifies the materials as public domain and applies No Copyright - United States.
rights.component: U-M digital text and public delivery
notes: NoC-US is jurisdiction-specific and is not itself a license or worldwide public-domain determination.

The legacy 2006 XML header says 'Where applicable, subject to copyright' and that other distribution restrictions may apply; that historical caution is preserved here rather than treated as overriding the current item-level NoC-US assertion.

access.accessMethod: The 2006 XML records a 230-image TIFF production layer and all 230 exact linked filenames, but no current image endpoint, supported request route for this set, or stable complete bundle was verified.
access.entityLabel: Historical U-M page-image set for AFW1075.0001.001
access.format: Historically described as 230 600 dpi TIFF G4 page images
notes: No image URL was invented from the legacy TIFF filenames.
Unknown current access does not deny the set's historical existence; U-M confirmation is required.

rights.basis: The current U-M item identifies its materials as public domain and applies No Copyright - United States, while the underlying 1914 edition is public domain in the United States. The historical TIFF set is not currently retrievable, and no separate image-specific license or delivery terms were verified.
rights.component: Historical U-M TIFF page-image production set for Spalding's 1914 edition
notes: NoC-US is jurisdiction-specific and is not itself a license or worldwide public-domain determination.
The legacy XML says that, where applicable, copyright and other distribution restrictions may apply; that caveat remains material until U-M confirms TIFF-specific scope and current delivery terms.
This record does not infer an open license or current public image access from historical filenames.

entities.description: The page-image production layer from which U-M created its 2006 OCR. The XML header describes 230 600 dpi TIFF G4 page images, and its PB elements preserve all 230 unique filenames from 00000001.tif through 00000230.tif. The XML contains 341 PB occurrences because parallel witness presentation serializes some source pages more than once. No current image endpoint or complete retrievable bundle was verified.
entities.label: Historical U-M page-image set for AFW1075.0001.001
notes: The 230 unique REF values form a complete consecutive filename run, 00000001.tif through 00000230.tif; there are 341 PB occurrences because 62 source images are reused in the XML's parallel-witness serialization.
This entity records a historically evidenced production layer. Its unknown survival status means that current retrievability of the TIFF set has not been established.

rights.basis: The current U-M item identifies its materials as public domain and applies No Copyright - United States, while the underlying 1914 edition is public domain in the United States. rights.component: Historical U-M TIFF page-image production set for Spalding's 1914 edition
notes: NoC-US is jurisdiction-specific and is not itself a license or worldwide public-domain determination.

bibliographic.citation: Mary Caroline Spalding, The Middle English Charters of Christ, Bryn Mawr College Monographs 15 (Bryn Mawr, PA: Bryn Mawr College, 1914).
bibliographic.title: The Middle English Charters of Christ

entities.description: Spalding's Bryn Mawr dissertation and monograph. The encoded selection retains title leaves, edited witness texts on pp. 3-81, and Appendix II on pp. 95-102, while omitting the preface and study, Appendix I, glossary, and back matter.
entities.label: Mary Caroline Spalding, The Middle English Charters of Christ (1914)

rights.basis: Published in the United States in 1914; the title leaf names Bryn Mawr College as copyright holder, but the term has expired.
rights.component: 1914 text, editorial matter, and typography

access.accessMethod: Download the complete scan, OCR, and scan metadata.
access.entityLabel: Internet Archive complete scan of Spalding 1914

localCopies.label: Complete Spalding 1914 PDF
notes: The complete carrier also includes identifying title leaves and non-target context omitted from the cataloged selection: front matter pp. iii-cxxiv, Appendix I pp. 83-94, glossary pp. 103-104, and residual back matter.

workPortion.label: Six cataloged works in the afw1075 selection

rights.basis: The source edition is public domain in the United States and no separate copyright assertion for the scan or OCR was found in the cached item metadata; this is not an affirmative open-license grant.
rights.component: Internet Archive page-image scan and OCR derivatives of the 1914 edition

entities.description: A complete public scan of the physical 1914 edition, with OCR and scan metadata.
entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Spalding 1914

rights.basis: The source edition is public domain in the United States and no separate copyright assertion for the scan or OCR was found in the cached item metadata; this is not an affirmative open-license grant.
rights.component: Internet Archive page-image scan and OCR derivatives of the 1914 edition

entities.description: A modern scholarly working-copy intermediary, provisionally typed manuscript_witness because the schema has no transcript type. Brown supplied transcripts of Short Charter witnesses Sloane 3292, Stowe 620, and Harley 116 and the Long Charter B text in Cotton Caligula A II. Location, physical form, and survival are unknown.
entities.label: Carleton Brown working transcripts used by Spalding

rights.basis: Spalding documents the transcripts' use, but their physical form, survival, ownership, publication history, and reuse terms were not verified.
rights.component: Carleton Brown's unpublished working transcripts and any modern annotations

entities.description: A modern scholarly working-copy intermediary, provisionally typed manuscript_witness because the schema has no transcript type. Rogers supplied the Short Charter in St John's B.15, Carta Libera in E.24, and the Carta Celestis Hereditatis extract in CUL Ff.6.34. Location, physical form, and survival are unknown.
entities.label: Alfred Rogers working transcripts used by Spalding

rights.basis: Spalding documents the transcripts' use, but their physical form, survival, ownership, publication history, and reuse terms were not verified.
rights.component: Alfred Rogers's unpublished working transcripts and any modern annotations

entities.description: A modern scholarly working transcript and manuscript description supplied to Spalding by the Magdalen College librarian, provisionally typed manuscript_witness because no transcript type exists. Location and survival are unknown.
entities.label: H. A. Wilson transcript of Magdalen St Peter-in-the-East 18e

rights.basis: Spalding documents the supplied transcript and description, but their physical form, survival, ownership, publication history, and reuse terms were not verified.
rights.component: H. A. Wilson's unpublished transcript, description, and any modern annotations

access.accessMethod: No public copy of Spalding's historical rotograph set or complete public facsimiles for the remaining exact source codices was verified; use the provider request routes recorded above.

rights.basis: Spalding documents use of rotographs for selected manuscript passages, but no surviving set, rights holder, license, or supply terms were identified.
rights.component: Spalding's historical rotograph photographs and any associated annotations

entities.description: A collective entity for rotographs of every printed manuscript text not routed through Brown, Rogers, Wilson, Macray, or Fehr. It is not evidence of whole-codex photography; each relation is scoped to the target passage. Location and survival are unknown.
entities.label: Spalding's manuscript rotographs

rights.basis: Spalding documents use of rotographs for selected manuscript passages, but no surviving set, rights holder, license, or supply terms were identified.
rights.component: Spalding's historical rotograph photographs and any associated annotations

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections about AFW1075.0001.001 and the exact filename range 00000001.tif-00000230.tif.
Request the complete 230-image set rather than only the target text pages if it remains available.
Record any current endpoint, inventory, delivery terms, and TIFF-specific rights statement only after verification.
openQuestions.question: Do the historical 230 U-M TIFF G4 page images still survive, and is there a current supported complete-set delivery or request route?

next_steps: Use the recorded institutional routes and request whole physical objects rather than target crops when practical.
Identify the target folios separately in each request.
Record quote, delivery format, image resolution, and publication/repository terms verbatim.
openQuestions.question: Can the holding institutions supply complete current digital facsimiles of the remaining exact source codices, and under what reuse terms?

next_steps: Ask each repository for a current foliation map or inspect complete current images.
Preserve both historical and current locators until the physical sequence is verified.
Check whether the differences reflect rebinding, missing leaves, or catalog scope rather than transcription error.
openQuestions.question: How do Spalding's historical folio references crosswalk to current catalog foliation for Ashmole 189, Add. C. 280, CUL Ii.3.26, Ff.2.38, and Ee.2.15?

next_steps: Search Bryn Mawr College archives and relevant personal papers.
Do not infer whole-manuscript coverage from the word rotograph.
If found, inventory each research carrier and connect it to the exact witness passage.
openQuestions.question: Do Spalding's historical rotographs or the Brown, Rogers, and Wilson working transcripts survive in an identifiable archive?

next_steps: Email Magdalen Archives with Spalding's shelfmark, the verso fragment incipit, and H. A. Wilson's 1912-era description.
Ask whether the fragment has been rebound, renumbered, transferred, or digitized.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current shelfmark and custodial description of Magdalen College St Peter-in-the-East 18e?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections to crosswalk the historical charter shelfmark.
Request the complete physical charter, including recto conveyance, dorse poem, seals, endorsements, and scale target.
openQuestions.question: What exact current Bodleian catalog identifier and reproduction route correspond to Kent Charter 233?

next_steps: Determine the authorized publication history of each exact witness text rather than equating manuscript age with United Kingdom public-domain status.
Seek institution-specific rights guidance before first publication of a witness text or supplied manuscript image in the United Kingdom.
Keep the recorded witness-text public-domain assertions limited to United States law until resolved.
openQuestions.question: Were all source-witness texts validly made available to the public before the United Kingdom's pre-1989 unpublished-work cutoff, or could any remain protected through 31 December 2039?

next_steps: Compare the full modern bibliography and manuscript stratigraphy rather than selecting one date silently.
Keep composition date separate from dates of surviving witnesses.
openQuestions.question: Should the Short Charter be dated to the late fourteenth century, as Spalding argued from linguistic and documentary evidence, or probably a century after the early-fourteenth-century analogues, as the modern METS introduction proposes?

reviewNotes: The cataloged artifact is an edition-level selection containing six abstract medieval works, not one medieval poem and not the complete 1914 dissertation.

Parallel witness streams are alternative textual witnesses, not separate intellectual works. Harley 6848 contributes variants and an attestation rather than a thirteenth complete Short Charter stream.
Long Charter C is discussed in omitted matter and is retained only as supporting context; it is not encoded as a continuous text.
Historical manuscript locators are preserved alongside current catalog locators where they conflict; none has been silently normalized.

The 230 historical U-M TIFF filenames constitute a distinct page-image carrier between the OCR encoding and Spalding's printed edition. The 341 PB occurrences are XML serialization events, not 341 separate images.

Underlying witness-text public-domain assertions are deliberately limited to United States law. United Kingdom status is left unresolved because official IPO guidance says some literary works unpublished at the end of 1988 remain protected through 31 December 2039.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a pinned copy of University of Michigan item AFW1075.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR and Level 1 TEI encoding of a sharply bounded selection from Mary Caroline Spalding's 1914 dissertation The Middle English Charters of Christ. Its header documents an immediate historical production layer of 230 600 dpi TIFF G4 page images: the XML contains 341 PB occurrences but only 230 unique REF filenames because parallel witness presentation reuses images. Current survival and retrievability of that TIFF set remain unknown. The encoding retains the title leaves, twelve complete Short Charter witness texts plus a separate Add Charter 5960 attestation block, seven Long Charter A-text witness streams, five B-text streams, and four Appendix II works; it omits the 124-page front matter, Appendix I, glossary, and residual back matter. Spalding's preface identifies four distinct transmission routes: collaborator transcripts, a Magdalen librarian's transcript, two earlier printed texts, and rotographs of all other printed manuscript passages. Complete public whole-codex facsimiles are cached for the Vernon manuscript and Bodleian MS Ashmole 61; complete scans of the 1914 edition, DIMEV v1.0, historical manuscript catalogs, current catalog records, and access documentation are also cached. No complete public facsimile was verified for the remaining source codices, and several historical/current foliation conflicts remain explicit.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval authors
catalogSummary.date: Fourteenth- and fifteenth-century works and witnesses; edited selection published 1914; electronic encoding published 2006
catalogSummary.region: England; the Short Charter is associated with northern English language, while the Long Charter is associated with Midland or southern English language
catalogSummary.title: The Middle English Charters of Christ

catalogingSubject.label: The Middle English Charters of Christ
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The intellectual and content unit selected from Mary Caroline Spalding's 1914 edition and encoded as U-M item AFW1075.0001.001: the Short Charter of Christ, the Long Charter of Christ, and four distinct Appendix II works. Parallel manuscript texts are alternative witnesses or versions of those works, not additional abstract works. The unit is neither a single medieval composition nor Spalding's complete dissertation.

assertion.notes: Recorded as Spalding's argued date rather than silently accepted as consensus.
This conflicts with Spalding's late-fourteenth-century proposal; both are retained.

dateStatements.display: Fifteenth century in British Library Add MS 21253
Fourteenth century in St John's College MS E.24

assertion.notes: The date of the recto legal document is a terminus, not proof that the dorse poem was entered in 1395.

dateStatements.display: No earlier than the 1395 conveyance on whose dorse the poem appears

extent.description: Six abstract medieval works occur within seven top-level source regions: title leaves; omitted prefatory matter; Short Charter texts; Long Charter texts; omitted Appendix I; Appendix II; and omitted back matter. Alternative witnesses and A/B textual versions are not counted as additional works. The XML contains 121 LG elements and 3,870 L elements, but those are representation metrics across alternative witnesses and include variant-only lines, so they are not a single-work verse extent.

formStatements.description: Four verse works and two prose works, with modern prose editorial matter.

scope.description: Primary language of two Appendix II works; Latin also occurs in headings, formulae, and embedded passages elsewhere

notes: This manifest catalogs a six-work edited selection, not one medieval work and not the complete 1914 dissertation.
Alternative manuscript witness streams and Long Charter A/B versions have zero additional intellectual-item count.
The XML's 3,870 L elements are a representation metric across alternative witnesses and variant-only lines, not the line count of a single poem.
Harley 6848 contributes variants and attestation details rather than a thirteenth continuous Short Charter witness stream.
The Long Charter C text is supporting context only because it is not continuously encoded in afw1075.
Appendix II contains four distinct works; Carta Celestis Hereditatis is incomplete and ends with an editorial ellipsis.

Keep composition date separate from surviving-witness dates.
Retain both sourced proposals until the conflict can be adjudicated.
openQuestions.question: Should the Short Charter's composition be dated to the late fourteenth century, as Spalding argued, or probably to the fifteenth century, as the modern METS introduction proposes?

openQuestions.question: How do Spalding's historical folio references crosswalk to current catalog foliation for Ashmole 189, Add. C. 280, CUL Ii.3.26, Ff.2.38, and Ee.2.15?

notes: This collective display does not assert common authorship; the six works may have different anonymous creators and transmitters.

scope.description: The six medieval intellectual works represented in the encoded selection
Selection, classification, collation, and arrangement of the Charter texts and Appendix II works

notes: Spalding reprinted Fehr's previously printed Fairfax text.
Spalding reprinted the Kent Charter text supplied through Macray's earlier publication.

summaries.value: An edition-level selection of medieval devotional works that imagine Christ's Passion and redemption through the form and language of legal charters. It presents the Short and Long Charters in multiple manuscript witnesses or textual versions, followed by four related Latin and Middle English works.

Six intellectual works are represented: the Short Charter of Christ; the Long Charter of Christ; Carta Domini Nostri Iesu Christi; Carta Libera; Carta Dei; and the incomplete prose Carta Celestis Hereditatis. The Short Charter has twelve continuous witness texts plus an attestation block and Harley 6848 variants. The Long Charter has seven encoded A-text witness streams and five encoded B-text streams; the C text is discussed but not continuously encoded.

The U-M encoding retains the edition's title leaves, printed texts on pp. 3-81, and Appendix II on pp. 95-102. It omits the 124-page prefatory study, Appendix I on pp. 83-94, the glossary on pp. 103-104, and residual back matter, representing some omitted spans with empty page placeholders.

Parallel print columns were serialized by OCR/TEI as consecutive DIV2 streams. Those streams are modeled as witnesses or versions beneath two abstract Charter works and assigned zero additional intellectual-item count.

notes: This is Spalding's publication title and the title of the encoded edition-level selection, not a collective medieval title supplied by the witnesses.
titles.value: The Middle English Charters of Christ

notes: The Latin title labels a Middle English verse work also described as the Kent Charter.
Spalding supplies this Latin title for an incomplete Middle English prose tract related to the Charter of Christ and to The Poor Caitiff.

work.author: Anonymous medieval authors
work.dateDisplay: Fourteenth- and fifteenth-century works and witnesses; edited selection published 1914; electronic encoding published 2006
work.displayTitle: The Middle English Charters of Christ
work.preferredTitle: The Middle English Charters of Christ

publication.embeddedTitle: THE MIDDLE ENGLISH CHARTERS OF CHRIST

work.regionDisplay: England; the Short Charter is associated with northern English language, while the Long Charter is associated with Midland or southern English language
work.summary: An edition-level selection of medieval devotional works that imagine Christ's Passion and redemption through the form and language of legal charters. It presents the Short and Long Charters in multiple manuscript witnesses or textual versions, followed by four related Latin and Middle English works.
